_disclaimer: ALL ITEMS IN THIS FILE ARE SYNTHETIC PLACEHOLDERS, hand-authored to exercise the LSAT Item contract. They are NOT real LSAT items and must NOT be used as study material. Real items must be sourced from official LSAC PrepTests with redistribution licensing (D-SR11). Every item carries SyntheticFlag=SYNTHETIC.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Nina borrowed her neighbor's ladder without asking, but returned it in perfect condition before the neighbor even noticed. Nina did nothing wrong.
items.Stem: Which one of the following principles, if valid, most helps to justify the reasoning above?
items.ChoiceA: People should always ask permission before borrowing.
items.ChoiceB: An action is wrong only if it causes harm to someone.
items.ChoiceC: Borrowing anything without asking is always wrong.
items.ChoiceD: Returning borrowed items promptly is a virtue.
items.ChoiceE: One should treat others' property as if it were one's own.
items.WhyWrongA: This cuts against the conclusion rather than supporting it.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. If wrongness requires harm and no harm occurred, then Nina did nothing wrong.
items.WhyWrongC: This contradicts the conclusion that Nina did nothing wrong.
items.WhyWrongD: Prompt return being virtuous does not establish that the borrowing was permissible.
items.WhyWrongE: A general care principle does not justify borrowing without asking.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Principle: A public official should recuse herself from any decision in which she has a personal financial stake.
items.Stem: Which one of the following judgments conforms most closely to the principle stated above?
items.ChoiceA: Councilor Ruiz discloses her BuildCo shares but votes on the contract anyway.
items.ChoiceB: Councilor Ruiz, who owns shares in BuildCo, abstains from the vote on BuildCo's city contract.
items.ChoiceC: Councilor Ruiz recuses herself from a vote on a park design she personally dislikes.
items.ChoiceD: Councilor Ruiz sells her BuildCo shares the day after the vote.
items.ChoiceE: Councilor Ruiz votes on BuildCo's contract because she believes BuildCo is the best choice.
items.WhyWrongA: Disclosure is not recusal; she still votes with a stake.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. She has a financial stake and recuses — exactly what the principle requires.
items.WhyWrongC: Personal dislike is not a financial stake.
items.WhyWrongD: Selling afterward does not satisfy recusal at the time of the decision.
items.WhyWrongE: Voting despite a financial stake violates the principle.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A teacher lets a student retake an exam the student missed because of a documented illness, but refuses a retake to a student who simply overslept.
items.Stem: Which one of the following principles is best illustrated by the teacher's decisions?
items.ChoiceA: Students should be accommodated for missed work only when the cause was beyond their control.
items.ChoiceB: Documented excuses are more persuasive than undocumented ones.
items.ChoiceC: Teachers should never permit exam retakes under any circumstances.
items.ChoiceD: Students should always be given a second chance on an exam.
items.ChoiceE: Students who oversleep deserve harsher penalties than other students.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Illness (beyond control) earns a retake; oversleeping (avoidable) does not — the principle both decisions share.
items.WhyWrongB: The distinction is control, not documentation as such.
items.WhyWrongC: This contradicts the granted retake in the first case.
items.WhyWrongD: This contradicts the refusal in the second case.
items.WhyWrongE: The teacher withholds a retake but imposes no extra penalty.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Principle: One should never make a promise that one does not intend to keep.
items.Stem: Which one of the following judgments conforms most closely to the principle stated above?
items.ChoiceA: Mia promised to finish the report by Friday and did so; Mia acted wrongly.
items.ChoiceB: Raj told a coworker he would attend her recital even though he had already decided not to go; Raj acted wrongly.
items.ChoiceC: Sara refused to promise something she was genuinely unsure she could do; Sara acted wrongly.
items.ChoiceD: Ana declined to make any promises at all that week; Ana acted wrongly.
items.ChoiceE: Tom broke a promise he had fully intended to keep when a family emergency arose; Tom acted wrongly.
items.WhyWrongA: Keeping a sincerely made promise is not wrong under the principle.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Raj made a promise he did not intend to keep — exactly the act the principle forbids — so judging him to have acted wrongly conforms.
items.WhyWrongC: Refusing to promise what one is unsure of is precisely what the principle would encourage.
items.WhyWrongD: The principle does not require making promises, so declining is not wrong.
items.WhyWrongE: The principle concerns intent when promising, not later breaking due to emergency; calling Tom wrong misapplies it.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The store issued a full refund to a customer whose blender failed after two years, even though the warranty had expired, because the customer had shopped there loyally for a decade.
items.Stem: Which one of the following principles, if valid, most helps to justify the store's decision?
items.ChoiceA: A business should treat every customer identically regardless of purchase history.
items.ChoiceB: Customers are entitled to a refund whenever a product they bought stops working.
items.ChoiceC: A product that fails after two years must have been defective at the time of sale.
items.ChoiceD: A business may extend goodwill beyond its formal obligations in order to reward long-term loyalty.
items.ChoiceE: A business should honor the terms of its warranties exactly as written.
items.WhyWrongA: Treating everyone identically would rule out a loyalty exception.
items.WhyWrongB: A blanket refund entitlement is far broader than the loyalty-based reason given.
items.WhyWrongC: Whether the blender was defective is not the reason the store gave.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. The refund went beyond the expired warranty specifically to reward loyalty; this principle licenses exactly that.
items.WhyWrongE: The warranty had expired, so strict adherence would forbid the refund.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Principle: An action is praiseworthy only if it is done from a sense of duty rather than for the sake of personal gain.
items.Stem: Which one of the following judgments conforms most closely to the principle?
items.ChoiceA: A volunteer helped at a shelter chiefly in hopes of impressing a future employer; her action was praiseworthy.
items.ChoiceB: A donor gave to charity mainly to secure a large tax deduction; his action was praiseworthy.
items.ChoiceC: A student studied hard specifically to win a cash scholarship; his effort was praiseworthy.
items.ChoiceD: A firefighter rescued a trapped child because saving lives was her sworn duty; her action was praiseworthy.
items.ChoiceE: A soldier obeyed an order purely out of fear of punishment; his action was praiseworthy.
items.WhyWrongA: Helping to impress an employer is motivated by gain, not duty.
items.WhyWrongB: A gift made for a tax break is done for gain, so the principle denies it praise.
items.WhyWrongC: Studying for a cash prize is for personal gain, so the principle withholds praise.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. The rescue was done from duty, not for gain, so calling it praiseworthy fits the principle.
items.WhyWrongE: Acting from fear of punishment is self-interested, not dutiful.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The committee disqualified the winning essay after learning it had been written with substantial help from a professional editor, even though the contest rules did not explicitly forbid outside editing.
items.Stem: Which one of the following principles, if valid, most helps to justify the committee's decision?
items.ChoiceA: A rule that is not written down cannot legitimately be enforced.
items.ChoiceB: Entries in a writing contest should reflect the entrant's own unaided work, whether or not the rules say so explicitly.
items.ChoiceC: Professional editing almost always improves the quality of a piece of writing.
items.ChoiceD: Contest rules should be interpreted as narrowly as the wording permits.
items.ChoiceE: The winner of a writing contest should be determined solely by the quality of the finished text.
items.WhyWrongA: This principle would forbid the very enforcement the committee undertook.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Disqualifying for outside help despite silent rules is justified by a principle that entries must be the entrant's own work regardless of explicit rules.
items.WhyWrongC: Whether editing improves writing does not justify disqualification.
items.WhyWrongD: Narrow interpretation would permit the editing the rules did not forbid.
items.WhyWrongE: Judging on finished quality alone would favor keeping the polished essay.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Principle: A person may keep a found object only if he or she has first made a reasonable effort to locate its owner.
items.Stem: Which one of the following judgments conforms most closely to the principle?
items.ChoiceA: Kai found an umbrella and turned it in to lost-and-found. Kai acted impermissibly.
items.ChoiceB: Finding a phone on the bus, Leo kept it at once without any attempt to find its owner. Leo acted permissibly.
items.ChoiceC: After finding a wallet, Dana posted notices and wrote to the address inside; when no owner appeared, she kept it. Dana acted permissibly.
items.ChoiceD: Mara found a valuable ring, made no effort to locate the owner, and kept it because it was worth a lot. Mara acted permissibly.
items.ChoiceE: Nina searched hard for a lost bag's owner, found him, and returned it. Nina acted impermissibly.
items.WhyWrongA: Turning an item in is not prohibited; calling it impermissible reverses the principle.
items.WhyWrongB: Keeping the phone with no effort to find the owner violates the principle.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Dana made a reasonable effort to find the owner before keeping the wallet, satisfying the principle's condition.
items.WhyWrongD: Value does not excuse the absence of any effort to find the owner.
items.WhyWrongE: Returning an item to its located owner is plainly consistent with the principle.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The teacher awarded a passing grade to a student despite several late assignments, because the student's serious illness that semester had been thoroughly documented.
items.Stem: Which one of the following principles most helps to justify the teacher's decision?
items.ChoiceA: A passing grade should always reflect a student's mastery of the course material.
items.ChoiceB: Teachers should give every student the benefit of the doubt in all matters.
items.ChoiceC: Any student who becomes ill should be exempted from all coursework.
items.ChoiceD: Assignment deadlines should be enforced strictly and uniformly for everyone.
items.ChoiceE: Students should not be penalized for lapses that are caused by circumstances genuinely beyond their control.
items.WhyWrongA: The grade was justified by the illness, not by demonstrated mastery.
items.WhyWrongB: A universal benefit-of-the-doubt rule is broader than, and not the basis for, this decision.
items.WhyWrongC: A blanket exemption from all coursework is far broader than the decision requires.
items.WhyWrongD: Strict, uniform enforcement would preclude the leniency the teacher showed.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. The documented illness is a circumstance beyond the student's control, and this principle says such lapses should not be penalized.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Principle: A journalist may publish a private individual's personal information only if doing so serves a clear public interest.
items.Stem: Which one of the following judgments conforms most closely to the principle above?
items.ChoiceA: A reporter published a private citizen's medical records purely because readers would find them titillating; the reporter acted wrongly.
items.ChoiceB: A reporter revealed that a mayor had secretly diverted public funds; the reporter acted wrongly.
items.ChoiceC: A reporter published details of a company's unsafe products in order to warn consumers; the reporter acted wrongly.
items.ChoiceD: A reporter published a private citizen's home address merely to satisfy public curiosity; the reporter acted rightly.
items.ChoiceE: A reporter withheld a private citizen's home address, publishing which would have served no purpose; the reporter acted wrongly.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Publishing private medical records for mere titillation serves no public interest, so the principle deems it wrong.
items.WhyWrongB: Exposing misuse of public funds clearly serves the public interest, so it is permitted, not wrong.
items.WhyWrongC: Warning consumers of unsafe products serves the public interest, so it is permitted.
items.WhyWrongD: Mere curiosity is not a clear public interest, so publishing was not right.
items.WhyWrongE: Withholding information whose publication serves no purpose is exactly what the principle would favor.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The city council rejected a developer's proposal to build on the wetland, even though the project would have created many jobs, citing the wetland's role in protecting the town from floods.
items.Stem: Which one of the following principles most helps to justify the council's decision?
items.ChoiceA: Job creation should be the primary goal of every major city-planning decision.
items.ChoiceB: When a natural feature protects a community from serious harm, that protection should outweigh the economic gains of destroying it.
items.ChoiceC: Development proposals should be approved unless they would clearly violate an existing law.
items.ChoiceD: Economic growth should always take precedence over environmental concerns.
items.ChoiceE: Wetlands are home to many rare species of plants and animals.
items.WhyWrongA: Prioritizing jobs would favor approving, not rejecting, the project.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. The council weighed flood protection above jobs; this principle endorses exactly that ordering when a feature guards against harm.
items.WhyWrongC: An approve-unless-illegal rule would have let the legal project proceed.
items.WhyWrongD: This principle would support the development, contradicting the council's decision.
items.WhyWrongE: The council cited flood protection, not species habitat, so this misses the stated reason.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Principle: One should criticize another person's work publicly only after first raising the concern with that person privately.
items.Stem: Which one of the following actions conforms most closely to the principle?
items.ChoiceA: Theo posted a harsh public critique and only afterward apologized privately. Theo's conduct was consistent with the principle.
items.ChoiceB: Priya publicly denounced her colleague's report without ever speaking to him about it first. Priya's conduct was consistent with the principle.
items.ChoiceC: Before publishing a critical review, Omar first discussed his concerns privately with the author; only then did he make the critique public. Omar's conduct was consistent with the principle.
items.ChoiceD: Lena publicly praised a colleague's work without speaking to her first. Lena's conduct violated the principle.
items.ChoiceE: Sam raised his concerns privately and, satisfied by the response, never made them public. Sam's conduct violated the principle.
items.WhyWrongA: Criticizing publicly first and apologizing later reverses the required order.
items.WhyWrongB: Public criticism with no prior private conversation violates the principle.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Omar raised the concern privately before going public, exactly the order the principle requires.
items.WhyWrongD: The principle governs criticism, not praise, so it does not apply here.
items.WhyWrongE: The principle permits, but does not require, going public; keeping it private is not a violation.
